title: Finish the Arkansas Valley Conduit Act
short_title: Finish the Arkansas Valley Conduit Act
plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for communities in Colorado to pay for a big water pipeline called the Arkansas Valley Conduit. The pipeline brings water from Pueblo Reservoir to towns in the Arkansas River Valley. The communities only have to pay 35 percent of what it cost to build the pipeline. They get 100 years to pay back the money they owe, and they don't have to pay any extra interest charges.
last_action_text: The Chair directed the Clerk to notify the Senate of the action of the House.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Finish the Arkansas Valley Conduit Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Arkansas Valley Conduit, Colorado
sections.text: This section changes an existing law called Public Law eighty seven dash five ninety. The changes remove old language about how much money must be paid for the Arkansas Valley Conduit water pipeline project in Colorado. Instead, it adds new rules for this water pipeline. The new rules say that to help communities and families who do not have reliable drinking water, the contract for this water pipeline must require payment of only thirty five percent of what the pipeline costs to build. This is true even if other federal water laws say something different. The contract payments must include money provided during construction from anyone other than the federal Secretary who manages this project. If the Secretary determines there is financial hardship, the remaining balance not covered by construction funding can be paid back over no more than seventy five years. The interest rate will be fifty percent of the rate set by the Secretary of the Treasury. This can also include money earned from contracts to use extra pipeline capacity or exchange contracts using facilities from the Fryingpan Arkansas project. The contract must also require the parties who sign it to take care of operating, maintaining, and replacing the pipeline.
